title: Simple probability
description: Practice finding probabilities of events, such as rolling dice, drawing marbles out of a bag, and spinning spinners.
topic_path: Topic A: Calculating and interpreting probabilities

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die. 

**What is $\text{P(roll greater than 4})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $2$ favorable outcomes (rolling a $5$ or $6$).

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\qquad \text{P(roll greater than 4}) = \dfrac{2}{6} \approx 0.33$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die.

**What is $\text{P(roll less than 4})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $3$ favorable outcomes $($rolling a $1$, $2$, or $3)$.

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\qquad \text{P(roll less than 4}) = \dfrac{3}{6} = 0.5$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die.

**What is $\text{P(not 5})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $5$ favorable outcomes $($rolling a $1, 2, 3, 4,$ or $6)$.

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\qquad \text{P(not 5}) = \dfrac{5}{6}\approx0.83$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die.

**What is $\text{P(roll a 2})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There is $1$ favorable outcome $($rolling a $2)$.

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\qquad \text{P(roll a 2}) = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.17$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die.

**What is $\text{P(roll an even number})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $3$ favorable outcomes $($rolling a $2$, $4$, or $6)$.

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\text{P(roll an even number}) = \dfrac{3}{6} =0.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die.

**What is $\text{P(roll greater than }6)$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $0$ favorable outcomes.

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\text{P(roll greater than }6) = \dfrac{0}{6} =0$

assessment_items.question_content:
You roll a fair $6$-sided die.

**What is $\text{P(not }3)$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $5$ favorable outcomes $(1$, $2$, $4$, $5$, and $6)$.

There are $6$ possible outcomes since it is a $6$-sided die.
$\text{P(roll not }3) = \dfrac{5}{6} \approx  0.83$

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. The spinner has $4$ equal sectors colored pink, purple, blue, and green. 

**What is $\text{P(blue})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There is $1$ favorable outcome (blue).

There are $4$ possible outcomes since there are $4$ equal sectors.
$\qquad  \text{P(blue}) = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$

options.alt: A circle split into fourths with each sector a different color: Blue, Purple, Pink, and Green. A spinner on the circle is pointing at the blue sector.

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. The spinner has $4$ equal sectors colored pink, purple, blue, and green.

**What is $\text{P(green})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]


[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There is $1$ favorable outcome (green).

There are $4$ possible outcomes since there are $4$ equal sectors.
$\qquad  \text{P(green}) = \dfrac{1}{4}=0.25$

options.alt: A circle split into fourths with each sector a different color: Blue, Purple, Pink, and Green. A spinner on the circle is pointing at the green sector.

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. The spinner has $4$ equal sectors colored pink, purple, blue, and green.

**What is $\text{P(not purple})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $3$ favorable outcomes (pink, green, or blue).

There are $4$ possible outcomes since there are $4$ equal sectors.
$\qquad \text{P(not purple}) = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$

options.alt: A circle split into fourths with each sector a different color: Blue, Purple, Pink, and Green. A spinner on the circle is pointing at the green sector.

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. Each sector shown has an equal area.

**What is $\text{P(not squirrel})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $4$ favorable outcomes (the $4$ sectors without squirrels).

There are $5$ possible outcomes since there are $5$ equal sectors.
$ \text{P(not squirrel}) = \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.8$

options.alt: A circle split into equal fifths with each sector having a different animal in it: squirrel, catepillar, rabbit, raccoon, and ladybug. A spinner on the circle is pointing at the ladybug sector.

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. Each sector shown has an equal area.

**What is $\text{P(beaver})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There is $1$ favorable outcome (the $1$ sector with a beaver).

There are $3$ possible outcomes since there are $3$ equal sectors.
$ \text{P(beaver}) = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.33$

options.alt: A circle split into equal thirds with each sector having a different animal in it: Beaver, Otter, and Turtle. A spinner on the circle is pointing at the beaver sector.

assessment_items.question_content:
You flip a fair coin.

**What is $\text{P(heads})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There is $1$ favorable outcome (heads).

There are $2$ possible outcomes (heads or tails).
$\qquad \text{P(heads}) = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a decimal!

assessment_items.question_content:
You flip a fair coin.

**What is $\text{P(tails})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There is $1$ favorable outcome (tails).

There are $2$ possible outcomes (heads or tails).
$\qquad \text{P(tails}) = \dfrac{1}{2}=0.5$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
Giovanna owns a farm. She is going to randomly select one animal to present at the state fair. She has $6$ pigs, $7$ chickens, and $10$ cows. 

**What is $\text{P(chicken})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $7$ favorable outcomes (the $7$ chickens).

There are $23$ possible outcomes (the $6 + 7 + 10 = 23$ total animals).
$\qquad \text{P(chicken}) = \dfrac{7}{23}\approx0.3$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
Leslie is a biologist. She is going to randomly select one animal from her lab to study. There are $5$ salamanders, $3$ crayfish, and $12$ minnows in her lab.

**What is $\text{P(salamander})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $5$ favorable outcomes (the $5$ salamanders).

There are $20$ possible outcomes (the $5 + 3 + 12 = 20$ total animals).
$\qquad \text{P(salamander}) = \dfrac{5}{20} = 0.25$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a decimal!

assessment_items.question_content:
Dhruv is at a car dealership. He is going to randomly select a vehicle to test drive. There are $13$ trucks, $8$ vans, and $4$ compact cars.

**What is $\text{P(compact car})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $4$ favorable outcomes (the $4$ compact cars).

There are $25$ possible outcomes (the $13 + 8 + 4 = 25$ total vehicles).
$\qquad \text{P(compact car}) = \dfrac{4}{25}=0.16$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
Greg has an MP3 player called the Jumble. The Jumble randomly selects a song for the user to listen to. Greg's Jumble has $6$ classical songs, $7$ rock songs, and $9$ rap songs on it. 

**What is $\text{P(not a rap song})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $13$ favorable outcomes (the $6 + 7 = 13$ songs that are *not* rap songs).

There are $22$ possible outcomes (the $6 + 7 + 9 = 22$ total songs).
$\qquad \text{P(not a rap song}) = \dfrac{13}{22}\approx0.59$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
Joel has an MP3 player called the Jumble. The Jumble randomly selects a song for the user to listen to. Joel's Jumble has $2$ classical songs, $13$ rock songs, and $5$ rap songs on it. 

**What is $\text{P(rock song or rap song})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $18$ favorable outcomes (the $13 + 5 = 18$ songs that are either rocks songs or rap songs).

There are $20$ possible outcomes (the $2 + 13 + 5 = 20$ total songs).
$\qquad \text{P(rock song or rap song}) = \dfrac{18}{20} = 0.9$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a decimal!

assessment_items.question_content:
You randomly draw a marble from a bag of marbles that contains $3$ blue marbles, $4$ green marbles, and $5$ red marbles.

**What is $\text{P(draw a blue marble})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $3$ favorable outcomes (the $3$ blue marbles).

There are $12$ possible outcomes (the $3 + 4 + 5 = 12$ total marbles).
$\qquad \text{P(draw a blue marble}) = \dfrac{3}{12} = 0.25$

options.alt: An image of 5 red marbles, 4 green marbles, and 3 blue marbles mixed up.

assessment_items.question_content:
You randomly draw a marble from a bag of marbles that contains $7$ blue marbles, $2$ green marbles, and $1$ red marble.

**What is $\text{P(not draw a blue marble})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $3$ favorable outcomes (the $2 + 1 = 3$ marbles that are either green or red).

There are $10$ possible outcomes (the $7 + 2 + 1 = 10$ total marbles).
$\qquad \text{P(not draw a blue marble}) = \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.3$

options.alt: An image of 1 red marble, 2 green marbles, and 7 blue marbles mixed up.

assessment_items.question_content:
You randomly draw a marble from a bag of marbles that contains $8$ blue marbles, $5$ green marbles, and $8$ red marbles.

**What is $\text{P(draw a blue or red marble})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $16$ favorable outcomes (the $8 + 8 = 16$ marbles that are either blue or red).

There are $21$ possible outcomes (the $8 + 5 + 8 = 21$ total marbles).
$\qquad \text{P(draw a blue or red marble}) = \dfrac{16}{21}\approx0.76$

options.alt: An image of 8 red marbles, 5 green marbles, and 8 blue marbles mixed up.

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. Each sector shown has an equal area.

There are $5$ possible outcomes since there are $5$ equal sectors.
$\qquad \text{P(shaded sector}) = \dfrac{2}{5} = 0.4$

options.alt: A circle split into fifths with 2 blue sectors and 3 white sectors. A spinner on the circle is pointing at the blue sector.

assessment_items.question_content:

**What is $\text{P(not shaded sector})$?**

There are $6$ favorable outcomes (the $6$ not shaded sectors).

There are $12$ possible outcomes since there are $12$ equal sectors.
$\qquad \text{P(not shaded sector}) = \dfrac{6}{12} = 0.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
You spin the spinner shown below once. Each sector shown has an equal area.

**What is $\text{P(beaver})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Jake is going to call one person from his contacts at random. He has $30$ total contacts. $16$ of those contacts are people he met at school.

**What is $\text{P(call a person from school})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $16$ favorable outcomes (the $16$ people Jake met at school).

There are $30$ possible outcomes (the $30$ total contacts).
$\qquad \text{P(call a person from school}) = \dfrac{16}{30} \approx0.53$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
Bruce is going to call one person from his contacts at random. He has $25$ total contacts. $20$ of those contacts  are from his neighborhood.

**What is $\text{P(call a person not from his neighborhood})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $5$ favorable outcomes (the $25 - 20 = 5$ people not from his neighborhood).

There are $25$ possible outcomes (the $25$ total contacts).
$\qquad \text{P(call a person not from his neighborhood}) = \dfrac{5}{25} = 0.2$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a decimal!

assessment_items.question_content:
Omar ordered his sister a birthday card from a company that randomly selects a card from their inventory. The company has $21$ total cards in inventory. $14$ of those cards are birthday cards.

**What is $\text{P(not a birthday card})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $7$ favorable outcomes (the $21 - 14 = 7$ cards that are not birthday cards).

There are $21$ possible outcomes (the $21$ total cards).
$\qquad \text{P(not a birthday card}) = \dfrac{7}{21} \approx0.33$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!

assessment_items.question_content:
You randomly draw a marble out of a bag that contains $20$ total marbles. $12$ of the marbles in the bag are blue.

**What is $\text{P(draw a blue marble})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $12$ favorable outcomes (the $12$ blue marbles).

There are $20$ possible outcomes (the $20$ total marbles).
$\qquad \text{P(draw a blue marble}) = \dfrac{12}{20} = 0.6$

options.alt: An image of 5 red marbles, 3 green marbles, and 12 blue marbles mixed up.

assessment_items.question_content:
You draw a card at random from a deck that contains $3$ black cards and $7$ red cards.

**What is $\text{P(draw a black card})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $3$ favorable outcomes (the $3$ black cards).

There are $10$ possible outcomes (the $3 + 7 = 10$ total cards).
$\qquad \text{P(draw a black card}) = \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.3$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a decimal!

assessment_items.question_content:
Cam can't figure out what to eat. He is going to randomly select a piece of fruit from his pantry. There are $4$ apples and $5$ bananas in his pantry.

**What is $\text{P(select an apple})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $4$ favorable outcomes (the $4$ apples).

There are $9$ possible outcomes (the $4 + 5 = 9$ total pieces of fruit).
$\qquad \text{P(select an apple}) = \dfrac{4}{9}\approx0.44$

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Avery is going to randomly select one student from her class to read a poem out loud. There are $15$ boys and $13$ girls in her class.

**What is $\text{P(boy})$?**  
*If necessary, round your answer to $2$ decimal places.*  
[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: $\text{Probability} = \dfrac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Number of possible outcomes}}$

There are $15$ favorable outcomes (the $15$ boys).

There are $28$ possible outcomes (the $15 + 13 = 28$ total students in the class).
$\qquad \text{P(boy}) = \dfrac{15}{28}\approx0.54$

answers.message: Make sure to express your answer as a simplified fraction!
